explanation: TRUE POSITIVE. The bug is confirmed by direct code tracing. __CallTracker_init (line 44) calls _setCallRes(creator, 1, reqId, true), which writes to _crBySeqNum[creator][1] and _crByReqId[reqId], but does NOT advance _seqNums[creator] (remains 0). Consequently, getSeqNum(creator) returns 1 (the fallback for 0), signaling to off-chain clients that seqNum=1 is the next valid sequence number. When the creator makes their first tracked call with seqNum=1 and a fresh reqId, _isReqReplay accepts it (nextExpected = 0 → 1, matches seqNum=1, advances _seqNums to 2), and _setCallRes unconditionally overwrites _crBySeqNum[creator][1] with the new CallRes. The init record at _crBySeqNum[creator][1] is silently lost. The documented invariant in ICallTracker.sol ("Each (caller, seqNum) uniquely maps to one reqId") is violated. Replay of the init call via (creator, seqNum=1, reqId_init) is permanently broken after the overwrite — _isReqReplay finds reqId_init in _crByReqId (not new), checks _crBySeqNum[creator][1] for matching reqId, gets reqId_new instead, and reverts with RequestKeyConflict. Additionally, any off-chain agent assuming init consumed seqNum=1 and starting at seqNum=2 will hit a SeqNumGap revert. Medium severity is appropriate: real invariant violation with off-chain reliability impact, but normal on-chain FIFO behavior continues to work correctly after the overwrite.
